steps.title: Identify what "and" is connecting
steps.content: The verb “determine” takes two parallel noun clauses as its objects: “how current-driven mixing varies across regions” and “what impact it has on the distribution of heat and nutrients in the ocean.” The word completing the blank ends the first clause and leads into “and.”

steps.title: Eliminate wrong choices and confirm the answer
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'regions,': A comma before “and” joining exactly two parallel clauses is incorrect — no punctuation is needed here. ✗
  • Choice B — 'regions:': A colon introduces a list or explanation; it cannot separate two coordinate clauses joined by “and.” ✗
  • Choice C — 'regions;': A semicolon joins independent clauses; the second element here is not an independent clause. ✗
  • Choice D — 'regions': No punctuation before “and” when joining two parallel noun clauses — this is the correct convention. ✓
That matches choice D — D is correct.
